title: Health Insurance Fundamentals
description: Distinguish health plan types, understand ACA provisions, and apply health insurance cost-sharing concepts

sections.title: Health Plan Cost-Sharing Terms
items: Premium: Monthly cost for coverage
Deductible: Annual out-of-pocket before insurance pays
Copayment: Fixed amount per service (e.g., $30 doctor visit)
Coinsurance: Percentage split (e.g., 80/20 after deductible)
Out-of-Pocket Maximum: Cap on annual cost-sharing; 2026 limit: $9,800 individual / $19,600 family

sections.title: Plan Type Details
items: HMO (Health Maintenance Organization): Closed network, requires PCP, referrals required, lowest premiums
PPO (Preferred Provider Organization): In-network preferred, no PCP/referrals required, out-of-network covered at higher cost
EPO (Exclusive Provider Organization): Must use network (like HMO), no referrals required, out-of-network not covered
POS (Point of Service): Hybrid HMO/PPO, referrals required for specialists, out-of-network covered with referral

sections.title: High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP)
sections.content: HDHPs have minimum deductible of $1,600 individual / $3,200 family (2024), maximum out-of-pocket of $8,050 individual / $16,100 family. They offer lower premiums but higher cost-sharing.

sections.title: Health Savings Account (HSA)
sections.content: HSA Triple Tax Advantage: (1) Tax-deductible contributions, (2) Tax-deferred growth, (3) Tax-free qualified withdrawals. Eligibility requires HDHP enrollment, no other health coverage, not on Medicare, not claimed as dependent.

sections.title: HSA Contribution Limits (2026)
items: Individual: $4,450
Family: $8,900
Catch-up (55+): Additional $1,000

sections.title: ACA Essential Health Benefits
items: Ambulatory care
Emergency services
Hospitalization
Maternity/newborn
Mental health
Prescription drugs
Rehabilitative services
Lab services
Preventive care
Pediatric (dental/vision)

sections.title: ACA Coverage Mandates
items: No lifetime/annual limits
Dependent coverage to age 26
No pre-existing condition exclusions
Preventive care with no cost-sharing

sections.title: Exam Tip: HSA Triple Tax
sections.content: Remember HSA Triple Tax: Tax-deductible in, Tax-free growth, Tax-free out (for medical). FSAs are use-it-or-lose-it while HSAs roll over and are portable.

sections.title: Summary
items: HMOs require network use and referrals; lowest cost but least flexibility
PPOs allow out-of-network care at higher cost; most flexibility
HSAs require HDHP enrollment and offer triple tax advantage
FSAs are use-it-or-lose-it; HSAs roll over and are portable
ACA metal tiers reflect actuarial value: Bronze (60%) to Platinum (90%)

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah is considering enrolling in a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) for 2024.  Which of the following statements accurately reflects the cost-sharing parameters of an HDHP?
options: The minimum annual deductible for family coverage is $1,400, and the maximum out-of-pocket expense is $15,000.
The minimum annual deductible for individual coverage is $1,600, and the maximum out-of-pocket expense is $8,050.
The minimum annual deductible for family coverage is $3,000, and the maximum out-of-pocket expense is $14,000.
There are no mandated minimum deductible or maximum out-of-pocket expenses for HDHPs.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is B. For 2024, HDHPs must have a minimum deductible of $1,600 for individual coverage and a maximum out-of-pocket expense of $8,050. The other options present incorrect values for either the minimum deductible or the maximum out-of-pocket expense.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to differentiate between individual and family coverage limits.

reveal.question: Define a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP).
reveal.answer: A High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) is a health insurance plan with a higher deductible than a traditional insurance plan.  In 2024, it must have a minimum deductible of $1,600 for individuals and $3,200 for families.  It also has a maximum out-of-pocket expense of $8,050 for individuals and $16,100 for families. HDHPs typically have lower premiums than traditional plans.

title: Disability Income Insurance
description: Calculate disability income needs and compare disability policy features including definitions
topics: Understand tax treatment of disability benefits

sections.title: The Need for Disability Insurance
sections.content: 1 in 4 workers become disabled before retirement. Average disability lasts over 34 months. Disability is the most underinsured risk. Income replacement need is generally 60-70% of gross income.

sections.title: Definition of Disability
items: Own-Occupation: Cannot perform YOUR occupation; can work in another field and collect benefits; best protection (highest cost)
Any-Occupation: Cannot perform ANY occupation for which suited; more restrictive; lower premiums
Split Definition: Own-occupation for 2-5 years, then any-occupation
Presumptive Disability: Automatic total disability for loss of sight, hands/feet, hearing/speech

sections.title: Optional Riders
items: Cost-of-Living Adjustment (COLA): Benefits increase with inflation (CPI); adds 20-40% to premium
Future Purchase Option: Buy additional coverage without evidence; triggered by income increases; critical for young professionals
Residual/Partial Disability: Benefits for reduced income even if able to work part-time
Return of Premium: Refunds premiums if not disabled; significant cost increase

sections.title: Residual Disability Formula
sections.content: Residual Benefit = [(Prior Income - Current Income) ÷ Prior Income] × Total Monthly Benefit. Example: Prior income $10,000, current income $4,000, total benefit $6,000. Residual = 60% × $6,000 = $3,600.

sections.title: Exam Tip: OAR Mnemonic
sections.content: Disability Definitions: Own-occupation (best), Any-occupation (restrictive), Residual/partial. Key rule: Pay premiums with after-tax dollars to receive tax-free benefits.

sections.title: Summary
items: Own-occupation definition is most favorable; any-occupation is restrictive
Longer elimination periods reduce premiums significantly
COLA rider protects against inflation during long-term disability
Individual policies are portable with tax-free benefits (if you pay)
Benefits taxable only if employer paid premiums as untaxed benefit

knowledgeCheck.question: John earns $100,000 annually and wants disability insurance to replace 70% of his income. His state taxes are 5%, and federal taxes are 20%. If his disability policy benefits are taxable, what monthly benefit should he apply for to meet his income replacement goal, rounded to the nearest hundred?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the desired annual benefit: $100,000 * 70% = $70,000. Then, calculate the after-tax income he needs to replace. His total tax rate is 5% + 20% = 25%. Since the benefits are taxable, he needs to receive enough to cover the taxes. Divide the target income by (1 - tax rate): $70,000 / (1 - 0.25) = $93,333.33. Finally, convert to monthly: $93,333.33 / 12 = $7,777.78. The question asks what amount needs to be applied for not the after tax amount. Multiply $7,777.78 by .75 = $5833.33
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to account for taxes when calculating the required benefit amount.

rows.label: Definition
values: Inability to perform the material and substantial duties of *your* regular occupation.
Inability to perform the duties of *any* occupation for which you are reasonably suited by education, training, or experience.

rows.label: Benefit Trigger
values: Easier to qualify for benefits; can receive benefits even if able to work in another field.
More difficult to qualify for benefits; must be unable to work in any suitable occupation.

reveal.question: How are disability insurance benefits taxed if the premiums were paid with after-tax dollars?
reveal.answer: The benefits are generally received income-tax free. If the employer pays the premiums, then the benefits are taxable.

title: Long-Term Care Insurance
description: Understand LTC coverage triggers, compare policy features, and apply tax treatment of LTC policies
topics: Apply tax treatment of LTC policies

sections.title: Understanding Long-Term Care
sections.content: Long-term care provides assistance with daily living activities for extended periods, including nursing home care, assisted living, home health care, and adult day care. 70% of people over 65 will need some LTC. Average nursing home costs $108,000/year (private room). Medicare does NOT cover custodial care.

sections.title: Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
sections.content: Most policies require inability to perform 2 of 6 ADLs to trigger benefits: Bathing, Dressing, Toileting, Transferring (moving in/out of bed), Continence, Eating. Cognitive impairment (Alzheimer's, dementia) triggers benefits even if ADLs intact.

sections.title: Policy Features
items: Benefit Amount: Daily or monthly benefit, typical $150-$400/day based on cost of care in your area
Elimination Period: Waiting period before benefits begin; 30, 60, 90 days common; longer period = lower premium
Care Settings Covered: Nursing home, assisted living, memory care, home health care, adult day care, hospice

sections.title: Simple vs Compound Inflation
sections.content: Simple inflation increases by fixed percentage of original benefit (e.g., $200/day × 5% = $10 increase annually). Compound inflation increases by percentage of current benefit ($200 → $210 → $220.50 → $231.53...).

sections.title: Tax-Qualified LTC Policy Requirements
items: No prior hospitalization required
2 of 6 ADLs or cognitive impairment trigger
90-day certification of condition
Following plan of care

sections.title: LTC Tax Treatment
sections.content: Premiums are deductible as medical expense (subject to 7.5% AGI floor) up to age-based limits. Benefits are tax-free up to per diem limit ($420/day in 2024) or actual care costs (unlimited).

sections.title: Partnership Policies
sections.content: Partnership policies protect assets from Medicaid spend-down. How it works: Buy state-approved partnership policy, receive policy benefits, if exhausted and need Medicaid, you can keep assets equal to benefits received. Example: $300,000 policy allows keeping $302,000 in assets instead of normal $2,000 Medicaid limit.

sections.title: Hybrid/Combination Policies
items: Life Insurance + LTC: Death benefit with LTC rider; use death benefit for LTC if needed; remaining death benefit to heirs
Advantages: Benefits used either way; no "use it or lose it"; single premium options
Considerations: Higher initial cost; may reduce life insurance to family

sections.title: Exam Tip: BEDICCT Mnemonic
sections.content: ADLs: Bathing, Eating, Dressing, (Incontinence) Continence, Continence, Transferring, Toileting.

sections.title: Summary
items: LTC triggers: inability to perform 2 of 6 ADLs OR cognitive impairment
Medicare does NOT cover custodial/long-term care
Compound inflation protection provides strongest benefit growth
Partnership policies protect assets from Medicaid spend-down
Hybrid policies combine life insurance with LTC benefits

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes a common trigger for Long-Term Care (LTC) insurance benefits?
options: Being unable to perform one Activity of Daily Living (ADL) for more than 30 days.
Being unable to perform at least two Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) for a period specified in the policy, typically 90 days.
A diagnosis of any chronic illness, regardless of the ability to perform ADLs.
Reaching the age of 70 and requiring any type of assistance with daily living.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Most LTC policies require the inability to perform at least two ADLs (Activities of Daily Living) or cognitive impairment for a specified period, often 90 days, before benefits are triggered. One ADL or a chronic illness diagnosis alone is typically not sufficient.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the ADL requirements and the waiting period.

rows.label: Benefit Increase
values: The benefit amount increases by a fixed percentage of the *original* benefit amount each year.
The benefit amount increases by a fixed percentage of the *previous year's* benefit amount each year.

rows.label: Growth Rate
values: Slower growth over time, especially in later years.
Faster growth over time due to compounding effect.

rows.label: Cost
values: Generally less expensive than compound inflation protection.
Generally more expensive than simple inflation protection.

reveal.question: What is the MAIN benefit of a Long-Term Care Partnership Program?
reveal.answer: Asset protection. Partnership programs allow individuals who purchase qualified LTC policies to protect a certain amount of their assets (usually equal to the amount of benefits paid out by the LTC policy) and still qualify for Medicaid if their LTC needs exceed their policy benefits. This means they can receive Medicaid assistance without having to deplete all of their assets.

title: Medicare and Social Security Health Benefits
description: Explain Medicare Parts A, B, C, and D coverage and calculate Medicare premiums including IRMAA surcharges
topics: Explain Medicare Parts A, B, C, and D coverage
Calculate Medicare premiums including IRMAA surcharges
Distinguish Medicare from Medigap and Medicare Advantage

sections.title: Medicare Eligibility
items: Age-Based: Age 65+, automatic if receiving Social Security
Initial Enrollment Period: 7-month window around 65th birthday (3 months before, birth month, 3 months after)
Disability-Based: After 24 months of SSDI benefits; immediate for ALS or ESRD

sections.title: Medicare Parts Overview
sections.content: Medicare Parts: A=Admitted (hospital), B=Bills (doctor), C=Choice (Advantage), D=Drugs

sections.title: Part A - Hospital Insurance
items: Covers: Inpatient hospital, skilled nursing facility (up to 100 days), home health care, hospice
Premium: $0 if 40+ quarters of work history
Deductible: ~$1,676 per benefit period (2026)
Coinsurance: Days 1-60 = $0; Days 61-90 = ~$419/day

sections.title: Part B - Medical Insurance
items: Covers: Outpatient care, physician services, preventive services, durable medical equipment, mental health
Standard Premium: ~$185/month (2026)
Deductible: ~$257/year
Coinsurance: 20% of Medicare-approved amount

sections.title: Part C - Medicare Advantage
sections.content: Private plans approved by Medicare that MUST include Part A and B coverage. Often includes Part D (prescription drugs) and extra benefits (dental, vision, hearing). Types include HMO (network required), PPO (out-of-network allowed), and PFFS (Private Fee-for-Service).

sections.title: Part D - Prescription Drugs (2026)
items: Deductible: Up to $590
Initial Coverage: Plan pays 75%, you pay 25%
Coverage Gap (Donut Hole): You pay 25% (Inflation Reduction Act closed the gap)
Catastrophic: You pay $0 (after $2,000 OOP spending)

sections.title: IRMAA - Income-Related Monthly Adjustment
sections.content: Higher-income beneficiaries pay more for Parts B and D. IRMAA is based on MAGI from 2 years prior. Planning consideration: Roth conversions or capital gains can trigger IRMAA.

sections.title: Medigap (Medicare Supplement)
items: Purpose: Fills gaps in Original Medicare (Parts A and B) - deductibles, coinsurance, copayments
Standardized plans (A, B, C, D, F, G, K, L, M, N)
Plan G is most popular (F no longer available to new enrollees after 2020)
Cannot be used with Medicare Advantage
Best time to buy: 6-month open enrollment starting at age 65 (guaranteed issue)

sections.title: Enrollment Periods and Penalties
items: Initial Enrollment Period (IEP): 7 months around 65th birthday
General Enrollment Period: January 1 - March 31; coverage starts July 1
Special Enrollment Period (SEP): When losing employer coverage; no penalty within 8 months
Part B Late Penalty: 10% premium increase per 12 months delayed; PERMANENT
Part D Late Penalty: 1% of base premium × months without creditable coverage; PERMANENT

sections.title: HSA and Medicare
sections.content: Cannot contribute to HSA once enrolled in ANY Medicare part. Stop HSA contributions 6 months before Medicare Part A enrollment (backdates to SSA effective date).

sections.title: Common Medicare Questions
rows: NO - Only skilled nursing (100 days max)

sections.title: Summary
items: Medicare Part A (hospital) usually $0 premium; Part B (medical) ~$185/month
IRMAA surcharges apply 2 years after high-income year
Part D catastrophic = $0 after $2,000 OOP (Inflation Reduction Act)
Late enrollment penalties are permanent - 10%/year for Part B
Cannot contribute to HSA after Medicare enrollment

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding Medicare Part C (Medicare Advantage)?
options: Medicare Advantage plans are administered directly by the federal government and offer identical coverage nationwide.
Medicare Advantage plans are private plans approved by Medicare that must include Part A and Part B coverage, but are not required to include Part D (prescription drug) coverage.
Medicare Advantage plans may offer extra benefits such as dental, vision, and hearing, but generally require beneficiaries to pay higher premiums than Original Medicare.
Medicare Advantage plans are only available to individuals with pre-existing health conditions who are ineligible for Original Medicare.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Medicare Advantage plans are indeed private plans approved by Medicare that MUST include Part A and B coverage. They often include Part D and extra benefits. While premiums can vary, they are not necessarily higher than Original Medicare when factoring in supplemental insurance costs.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the role of private insurance companies in Medicare Advantage.

reveal.question: Briefly describe the key differences between HMO, PPO, and PFFS Medicare Advantage plans.
reveal.answer: HMO (Health Maintenance Organization): Requires you to stay within the plan's network of providers. PPO (Preferred Provider Organization): Allows you to see out-of-network providers, but usually at a higher cost. PFFS (Private Fee-for-Service): Determines how much it will pay doctors, hospitals, and providers, and how much you must pay when you get care. Not all PFFS plans have a network.

sections.title: Medicare vs. Medigap vs. Medicare Advantage

rows.label: Coverage
values: All of A & B, often D + extras
